track_listing.details: The Killing Of The Buffalo; LP-Chinatown

transcript.text: ...car and now both mother and daughter are doing well. And that's Radio 1 News at two and a half minutes past ten.
On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
TV on the radio here, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
And this evening the bulk of our program is taken up by 55 minutes of absolute excellence.
The type of performance that always emanates from any stage anywhere in the world when it's occupied by David Coverdale and Whitesnake.
We've got some classic cuts by Racing Cars and Led Zeppelin, among others,
and new releases by Thin Lizzy, John Anderson, Van Morrison, Black Axe,
Vardis and the Atlanta Rhythm Section.
After getting our Friday Night Connection, the answer to last week's and a new one.
But this evening, we start with Ted Nugent.
And that was Thin Lizzy and a track from their new album called Chinatown.
[Music]
[Music]
The track you hope was called Genocide.
And I love the video that they put together to promote the single,
which is a big hit, called Killer on the Loose.
It's really threatening.
Prior to that, you heard something by Ted Newton
from the album called Ted Newton.
The track was Storm Trooper.
I played that especially for
Cy Hayes and Alf Wesme Lake Hale
and Nick Byrne, who live in Altrincham, Cheshire.
And also for Carol, alias Winifred Jones,
who lives in Kenton in Newcastle upon Tyne.
I wanted me to dedicate it to a fellow that she met
when Pink Floyd were on in London.
She met him, he's from Leeds,
and they actually got together in Trafalgar Square.
but now they keep missing each other.
I'm also a player for Andy Gonzo-Taylor,
who lives at [redacted address], Dean Road, in Bolton, in Lancashire.
Twelve and a half minutes after ten, this is John Anderson.
I'm Matt Morrison. It's a track from his new album,
the album that's called Common One.
It came out recently on Mercury.
The track you heard was Satisfy.
Quite an amazing album, actually, in many ways,
because there are very few tracks on it.
There's a 13-minute track on it and a 15-minute track as well.
quite enjoyed watching him on the television the other night
that was really a good concert though
I must say I've always had a love-hate relationship with his music
sometimes I love it, other times I just
cannot stand it
and I don't really know why
prior to that you heard John Anderson and his first solo effort
since leaving Yes
it's a single that's coming out very shortly, it's called Summer Born
and I was asked
to play it for a load of Yes freaks
who go to Hall's house
Wolverston School, somewhere near Ipswich
it says here
and that's Sean and Si and Wayne and Tim and Billy and Bruce and John
and cheers to the Lottie-up.
Can I prevail upon your musical choice at the moment?
Do you remember the year 1970, a decade ago?
Well, if there's a particular piece of music from that decade
that would bring back some memories to you,
the last Friday in October,
we're going to be doing another of our occasional flashback programmes
that will be featuring the music of 1970.
So if there's something from that particular year
that you'd like to hear on the Friday Rock Show,
just jot it down on a postcard and send it to
The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
And we'll put the programme together, and maybe you'll hear your particular favourite.
This is Black Axe.
A couple of good singles there by a couple of good British young bands.
The latter one was by Vardis. It's a single called Let's Go. It's out on Logo.
And prior to that, you heard Black Axe. They're out on the metal label, and that was called Red Lights.
My little card here from Marie Haywood, who lives at [redacted address], Whitchurch in Shropshire.
Dear Tom, would you please play a record for my great boyfriend, Tim Barber, who I love very much.
Please would you tell him that I don't really get jealous about him listening to the Friday Rock Show,
well, maybe just a bit, and I don't mind as long as he's with me.
Also tell him that I love him, as he's going to listen to you. Do you hear that, Tim?
You don't know how jealous I get, says Marie.
I get really jealous of you. I can be sitting talking to him,
and all of a sudden he will say, this is Whitesnake, or whatever band it is.
Please play any record for him. He won't mind whatever it is, as long as it's not Quo.
Thanks very much, Marie Hayward.
Well, Marie, unfortunately, you're going to lose him for 55 minutes,
because that is the length of time that Whitesnake will be on the air later.
Here's a nice, delicate track, though.
It came out in 1976.
[Music]
Fabulous feel, that band.
I really do enjoy listening to them.
The Atlanta Rhythm Section.
It's a track from their brand-new album.
The album's called The Boys from Doraville.
It's out on Polydor.
The track was Try My Love.
And before that, you heard something from one of my all-time favorite albums.
I'm sure I say that too often.
An album that came out in 1976 and still bounces onto my turntable every once in a while.
The album called Downtown Tonight by an ace band who never really made it.
A band by the name of Racing Cars.
The track was Hard Working Woman.
And T. Wilson, the wise old sage that he is, told me today that Morty,
the man that used to be the lead singer with Racing Cars,
has got a solo album out at the moment and we're going to dig it out and play you some of it.
Because he's got a great voice.
20 minutes now before 11 o'clock here on the Friday Rock Show.
It's time for the Friday Night Connection.
Last week, we played you Tequila Sunrise by the Eagles from the LP Their Greatest Hits 7175.
Whiskey Rock-A-Rolla from Lynyrd Skynyrd, the LP Golden Platinum.
Bottle of Red Wine by Eric Clapton from the LP Clapton.
I think everybody got it right. I didn't see one wrong answer when I was going through the cards this afternoon.
The answer obviously had to be Tequila, Whiskey, Red Wine, Booze.
But the person who gets themselves the £10 record token
is a gentleman by the name of Keith Hind, H-Y-N-D,
at [redacted address], Cowdenbeath, Fife in Scotland, KY49RT.
Buy yourself a bottle of ten-year-old malt.
Well, no, you can't, actually, because it's a record token, Keith.
Congratulations. You've put together a very tasty connection.
Three bits of music. The connection's in there somewhere.
Could be in the name of the group, the name of the track that we're playing,
the name of the band. It could be anywhere.
I just tell you that it's in there definitely somewhere.
[Music]
Friday Night Connection for tonight, if you think you know what the answer is,
and it's in there somewhere.
Put it down on a postcard, any form of communication you like,
and send it to the Friday Night Connection,
the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
Don't forget to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well,
and congratulations to Keith Hine for that really tasty one.
Oh, incidentally, if you'd like to put on your letter or communication,
your choice for Flashback 70
you can jot that down as well
and save yourself a stamp, okay?
I really look forward to your letters every week
because they always make interesting reading
and it's nice to know what you actually like.
It's 2 minutes to 11
and now we're going to spend 55 minutes
in the company of an expectant and ready crowd
as recorded at Reading.
And the people who are going to hit the stage
are the people who closed this year's Reading Festival.
David Coverdale and Whitesnake.
ain't gonna cry no more love hunter my good friend bernie marsden on the guitar i think you do know
this song it's pointless we're saying if you know it sing it let's sing this together i hope you
enjoy it a white snake at running the tracks you heard fool for your loving ain't no love in the
heart of the city mistreated love hunter ain't gonna cry no more and walking in the shadow of
the blues plus sweet talker they really are a tight unit and a an absolutely great band the
Sound mix was done by Mike Robbo-Robinson,
and the production was done by Tony Wilson.
Next week, we're going to return to normal,
if you like to phrase it that way.
We'll have Girls' School,
a repeat of a set we recorded by them some time ago,
and also an archive session that, as yet, we haven't chosen.
Rock on, rock on, radio on.
[Music]
Led Zeppelin and the track from In Through the Outdoor,
the track called All of My Love.
When I play that, especially for a gentleman
who's going to be 26 in just one minute's time.
A gentleman by the name of James Birchend
who lives in Greys in Essex.
And today was the day they put Bonzo into the ground in Worcestershire.
I couldn't be there because I had to put the show together
along with Tony Wilson,
but I did appreciate the invitation to the funeral.
And that's it for this edition of the Friday Rock Show.
Thanks for your company.
I trust you enjoyed the program.
I thought the music was great, myself.
On Rock on Saturday tomorrow,
the specials Michael Schenker and Rockpile.
That's between 4 and 7.30.
Thanks to Steve, my engineer tonight,
for pressing all the right buttons
and making sure it all works.
Tony Wilson with now Waffle.
I mean, speak. Sorry.
A special goodnight to all unloved teddy bears.
Oh, romantic little fella.
Following the time signal for 12 midnight,
the 275-285 transmitters will be closing down
till first thing tomorrow morning.
I'm Tommy Vance.
Wherever you are,
I hope you have a great rest of the night.
From all of us on Radio 1,
goodnight and God bless.
It's midnight and we welcome VHF listeners
who've now joined us on Radio 2.
This is Tim Gutton in the Midnight Newsroom.
